diseases.name: Influenza (Flu)
diseases.description: A highly contagious viral infection that attacks your respiratory system, including nose, throat and lungs. It is common during seasonal cycles.
causes: Influenza viruses (primarily types A and B) spreading through droplets when infected people cough, sneeze, or talk.
Touching surfaces contaminated with the virus and then touching your eyes, nose, or mouth.
prevention: Get an annual flu vaccination.
Wash your hands frequently with soap and water.
Avoid close contact with people who are sick.
recommendations: Get plenty of rest to help your body fight the infection.
Drink lots of fluids (water, broth, herbal tea) to prevent dehydration.
Use over-the-counter pain relievers like acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and aches.
Consult a doctor for antiviral prescription medications if symptoms are severe.

diseases.name: COVID-19
diseases.description: An infectious respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, which can lead to mild to severe symptoms and affect multiple organ systems.
causes: SARS-CoV-2 virus transmitted through respiratory droplets, aerosols, or close contact.
Inhaling droplets from an infected individual coughing, sneezing, or speaking.
prevention: Stay up-to-date with COVID-19 vaccinations.
Wear masks in crowded or poorly ventilated indoor spaces.
Maintain physical distancing from others.
Wash hands frequently and use hand sanitizer.
recommendations: Self-isolate to prevent spreading the virus to others.
Monitor your oxygen levels and symptoms closely.
Seek immediate medical attention if you experience difficulty breathing, chest pain, or blue lips.
Rest, stay hydrated, and take fever reducers if symptoms remain mild.

diseases.name: Common Cold
diseases.description: A mild viral infection of the nose and throat, characterized by nasal congestion, sneezing, and runny nose, typically resolving in 7-10 days.
causes: Over 200 strains of viruses, most commonly Rhinoviruses.
Airborne droplets or direct contact with secretions of an infected person.
prevention: Wash hands frequently and avoid touching your face.
Disinfect frequently touched surfaces.
Avoid sharing cups, utensils, or towels with sick individuals.
recommendations: Stay warm and rest.
Inhale steam or use saline nasal sprays to relieve congestion.
Stay hydrated with water or warm soups.
Sargle with warm salt water to soothe a sore throat.

diseases.name: Migraine Headache
diseases.description: A neurological condition characterized by intense, throbbing headaches, often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound.
causes: Genetic factors and environmental triggers affecting brain activity and blood vessels.
Triggers like stress, hormonal changes, certain foods, lack of sleep, or bright lights.
prevention: Identify and avoid known migraine triggers.
Maintain a consistent sleep, eating, and exercise schedule.
Practice stress management techniques (e.g., meditation, yoga).
recommendations: Rest in a quiet, dark, and cool room when an attack begins.
Apply a cold compress or ice pack to your forehead or neck.
Take prescribed triptans or over-the-counter pain relievers early in the attack.
Stay hydrated and avoid caffeine spikes.

diseases.name: Bronchial Asthma
diseases.description: A chronic condition that affects the airways in the lungs, causing them to become inflamed, narrow, and produce extra mucus, making breathing difficult.
causes: A combination of genetic predisposition and environmental exposure to allergens.
Triggers such as pollen, dust mites, pet dander, exercise, cold air, or smoke.
prevention: Avoid triggers like cigarette smoke, dust, and pollen.
Use prescribed preventative inhalers consistently.
Get vaccinated against respiratory infections.
recommendations: Always carry a quick-relief rescue inhaler (albuterol).
Monitor your breathing using a peak flow meter if advised.
Follow a customized Asthma Action Plan created with your doctor.
Seek emergency medical help if breathing difficulty rapidly worsens.

diseases.name: Type 2 Diabetes
diseases.description: A chronic metabolic condition characterized by high levels of blood glucose, occurring when the body becomes resistant to insulin or doesn't make enough.
causes: Insulin resistance where body cells do not respond effectively to insulin.
Genetic predisposition, overweight, physical inactivity, and poor diet.
prevention: Maintain a healthy body weight.
Engage in regular physical activity (at least 150 minutes per week).
Eat a balanced diet high in fiber and low in refined sugars.
recommendations: Monitor blood sugar levels regularly.
Take prescribed oral medications or insulin as directed.
Schedule regular checkups to monitor kidney, eye, and cardiovascular health.
Work with a nutritionist to create a balanced meal plan.

diseases.name: GERD (Acid Reflux)
diseases.description: Gastroesophageal reflux disease occurs when stomach acid frequently flows back into the tube connecting your mouth and stomach, irritating the lining.
causes: Weakness or relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES).
Hiatal hernia, obesity, pregnancy, smoking, or eating large meals right before lying down.
prevention: Avoid trigger foods like spicy, fatty, acidic foods, caffeine, and chocolate.
Eat smaller, more frequent meals.
Do not lie down for at least 3 hours after eating.
Elevate the head of your bed by 6 inches.
recommendations: Take antacids, H2 blockers, or proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) as recommended by a doctor.
Avoid tight-fitting clothing around the waist.
Quit smoking, as nicotine weakens the esophageal sphincter.
Maintain a healthy weight.

diseases.name: Hypertension (High Blood Pressure)
diseases.description: A common condition in which the long-term force of the blood against your artery walls is high enough that it may eventually cause heart disease or stroke.
causes: Primary hypertension: Develops gradually over many years with no identifiable cause.
Secondary hypertension: Caused by conditions like kidney disease, thyroid problems, or medications.
Risk factors include age, family history, high salt intake, stress, and lack of exercise.
prevention: Limit sodium (salt) intake in your diet.
Exercise regularly and manage stress.
Limit alcohol consumption and avoid smoking.
recommendations: Monitor your blood pressure at home regularly.
Take prescribed blood pressure medications consistently.
Adopt the DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet.
Consult your cardiologist for routine heart checks.

diseases.name: Food Poisoning
diseases.description: An illness caused by eating contaminated, spoiled, or toxic food, resulting in acute gastrointestinal distress.
causes: Bacteria (such as Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria), viruses (Norovirus), or parasites in undercooked or improperly stored food.
Poor hygiene during food preparation.
prevention: Keep hands, utensils, and food preparation surfaces clean.
Separate raw meats from other foods.
Cook foods to safe internal temperatures.
Refrigerate perishable foods promptly within 2 hours.
recommendations: Drink plenty of electrolyte solutions, water, or broth to replace lost fluids.
Avoid solid foods for a few hours until the stomach settles.
Gradually reintroduce bland foods like bananas, rice, applesauce, and toast (BRAT diet).
Seek medical care if you show signs of severe dehydration, high fever, or blood in stool.

diseases.name: Allergic Rhinitis (Allergies)
diseases.description: An allergic response causing itchy eyes, sneezing, runny nose, and nasal congestion, triggered by indoor or outdoor allergens.
causes: Hypersensitivity of the immune system to harmless substances like pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or mold spores.
prevention: Keep windows closed during high pollen seasons.
Use allergen-proof covers on pillows and mattresses.
Wash hands after petting animals.
Keep indoor humidity low to prevent mold.
recommendations: Use over-the-counter antihistamines or nasal corticosteroid sprays.
Perform nasal irrigation using a saline rinse (e.g., neti pot).
Avoid outdoor activities in the early morning when pollen counts are highest.
Consider allergy shots (immunotherapy) if symptoms are severe and chronic.

diseases.name: Gastroenteritis (Stomach Flu)
diseases.description: An inflammation of the stomach and intestines, typically caused by a viral infection, resulting in vomiting, diarrhea, and cramps.
causes: Viruses such as Norovirus or Rotavirus spreading via contaminated food, water, or contact with infected individuals.
prevention: Wash hands thoroughly, especially after using the restroom or changing diapers.
Disinfect contaminated surfaces immediately.
Avoid raw or undercooked foods in regions with outbreaks.
recommendations: Hydration is key; drink oral rehydration salts (ORS), sports drinks, or diluted juices.
Eat small amounts of bland foods once vomiting stops.
Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, and highly seasoned foods.
Avoid taking anti-diarrheal medications without consulting a doctor, as they can prolong some infections.

diseases.name: Acute Bronchitis
diseases.description: An inflammation of the lining of your bronchial tubes, which carry air to and from your lungs, often developing after a cold or flu.
causes: Usually viruses (the same ones that cause colds and flu); occasionally bacteria.
Exposure to tobacco smoke, dust, fumes, or air pollution.
prevention: Avoid cigarette smoke and air pollutants.
Wear a mask when exposed to high dust or chemical levels.
Get seasonal flu shots.
recommendations: Use a humidifier or inhale steam to loosen mucus.
Rest and drink warm liquids.
Take cough suppressants if the cough keeps you awake at night.
If wheezing is present, a doctor may prescribe an inhaled bronchodilator.

diseases.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
diseases.description: An infection in any part of your urinary system, most commonly involving the bladder and the urethra.
causes: Bacteria (typically E. coli) entering the urinary tract through the urethra and multiplying in the bladder.
Risk factors include female anatomy, sexual activity, and certain types of birth control.
prevention: Drink plenty of water to help flush out bacteria.
Wipe from front to back after urinating or having a bowel movement.
Urinate shortly after sexual intercourse.
Avoid irritating feminine products.
recommendations: Consult a doctor immediately for prescription antibiotics (UTIs rarely clear up without them).
Use a heating pad on your abdomen to relieve cramping.
Drink cranberry juice, though evidence of its effectiveness is mixed, hydration is always good.
Complete the full course of antibiotics even if symptoms improve.

diseases.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
diseases.description: A mental health condition characterized by persistent, excessive, and unrealistic worry about everyday things, which can manifest physically.
causes: A complex interaction of genetics, brain chemistry, personality, and life events/stressors.
prevention: Incorporate regular aerobic exercise into your routine.
Limit caffeine and alcohol, which can worsen anxiety.
Practice relaxation exercises daily.
recommendations: Engage in Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) with a licensed therapist.
Use deep breathing techniques during acute anxiety spikes.
Establish a solid sleep routine.
Consult a medical professional regarding prescription selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or beta-blockers.

diseases.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
diseases.description: A potentially serious sleep disorder in which breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep due to throat muscles relaxing.
causes: Relaxation of throat muscles blocking the airway during sleep.
Risk factors include obesity, thick neck circumference, narrowed airway, male sex, and alcohol use.
prevention: Maintain a healthy weight.
Avoid alcohol and sleeping pills, which relax throat muscles.
Sleep on your side instead of your back.
recommendations: Use a CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) machine as prescribed by a sleep specialist.
Consider oral appliances designed to keep the throat open.
Avoid smoking, as it increases inflammation and fluid retention in the upper airway.
Discuss surgical options with your doctor if other treatments fail.
